title: Steak au Poivre
subtitle: Pepper-crusted steak with a quick cognac and cream pan sauce
description: Steak au poivre is a French bistro classic: a thick steak pressed into coarsely cracked black pepper, seared hard in butter and oil, then rested while a rapid pan sauce of cognac, stock, and cream is made from the same pan. The whole dish takes under twenty minutes. The pepper crust is not a suggestion — press firmly so the peppercorns embed into the surface and stay during searing rather than scattering into the pan.
sourceNotes: Steak au poivre is a fixture of French bistro and brasserie menus, documented since the early twentieth century. The cognac flambé version appears in Escoffier's Le Guide Culinaire (1903) adaptations and is standard in French cookery texts through the mid-twentieth century.

recipeTools.notes: Cast iron or stainless steel — not non-stick. Non-stick cannot withstand the high heat needed for steak and will not develop the fond required for the sauce.
For checking doneness: 52 °C = rare, 57 °C = medium-rare, 63 °C = medium.
Or a zip-lock bag and rolling pin — for cracking the peppercorns coarsely.

glossaryTerms.definition: The browned residue on the pan base after searing steak. Deglazing with cognac or stock lifts it into the sauce, adding depth and colour.
Leaving cooked meat off the heat before cutting. Juices redistribute back through the muscle fibres rather than running out onto the plate.

ingredients.notes: About 200g each, 3–4 cm thick — sirloin or ribeye also work
Or brandy — use a generous measure

steps: Crack the peppercorns coarsely — in a pestle and mortar or in a zip-lock bag with a rolling pin. You want rough chunks, not powder.
Pat the steaks dry with kitchen paper. Season both sides with salt. Press both sides firmly into the cracked pepper so it adheres — do not shake off the excess.
Heat a heavy frying pan over the highest heat possible for 2 minutes until smoking. Add the oil, then the butter. Once the butter foam subsides, lay the steaks in the pan away from you.
Sear without moving for 2–3 minutes per side for medium-rare on a 3 cm steak. Use a thermometer if unsure — 57 °C for medium-rare. Transfer to a warm plate and rest for 5 minutes.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the shallot to the pan and cook for 1 minute. Remove the pan from the heat and carefully pour in the cognac — step back as it may flame. Return to the heat and let it bubble for 30 seconds.
Add the hot beef stock and scrape up all the fond from the base. Let it reduce by half, about 2 minutes. Pour in the cream and simmer for 2–3 minutes until the sauce coats the back of a spoon.
Remove from the heat, add the final 10g of butter, and swirl to incorporate. Taste and adjust seasoning.
Serve the steaks immediately with the sauce spooned over.

content.text: The pan must be at its absolute hottest before the steaks go in — no exceptions. A correctly hot pan sears, seals, and colours in 2–3 minutes per side; an insufficiently hot pan steams. Dry the steaks with kitchen paper and let them come to room temperature before cooking. A cold steak from the fridge cools the pan temperature on contact.

Crack {{black-peppercorns}} of black peppercorns coarsely. Pat the steaks dry, season with salt, and press both sides firmly into the cracked pepper. Heat a heavy frying pan over the highest heat for 2 minutes. Add {{olive-oil}} of oil and {{butter-unsalted}} of butter. Once the foam subsides, sear the steaks for 2–3 minutes per side for medium-rare. Transfer to a warm plate and rest for 5 minutes — the

. Reduce by half, then add {{double-cream}} of cream. Simmer for 2–3 minutes until the sauce coats a spoon. Remove from the heat, swirl in the final 10g of butter, taste and adjust seasoning. Serve with sauce spooned over the steaks.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Two things that commonly go wrong.

attrs.solution: Not pressed in firmly enough, or the pan was moved before a good crust had formed. Press the peppercorns in with your palm before cooking, and resist the urge to move the steak for the first 90 seconds — let the crust set before lifting.

The cream was boiled too aggressively after the cognac was added. Reduce the heat once the cream goes in and let it thicken at a gentle simmer. If the sauce splits, whisk in a splash of cold stock off the heat.
